title.raw: 卷五十九 張湯傳 Volume 59: Zhang Tang

sentences.zh: 張湯，杜陵人也。

translations.literal: Zhang Tang was a man of Duling.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Tang came from Duling.

sentences.zh: 父為長安丞，出，湯為兒守舍。

translations.literal: His father was assistant magistrate of Chang'an; when he went out, Tang as a child guarded the house.
translations.idiomatic: His father served as assistant magistrate of Chang'an. Whenever the father left on business, the boy Tang stayed behind to watch the house.

sentences.zh: 還，鼠盜肉，父怨，笞湯。

translations.literal: Returning, a rat had stolen meat; the father was resentful and flogged Tang.
translations.idiomatic: When he came home, he found a rat had stolen the meat. Angry, he beat Tang with a stick.

sentences.zh: 湯掘熏得鼠及余肉，劾鼠掠治，傳爰書，訊鞫論報，並取鼠與肉，具獄磔堂下。

translations.literal: Tang dug and smoked out the rat and the remaining meat, impeached the rat, tortured and examined it, transmitted the indictment, interrogated and judged and reported, and together took the rat and meat, completed the case, and dismembered them below the hall.
translations.idiomatic: Tang excavated the burrow, smoked the rat out, recovered the leftover meat, then drew up a formal accusation against the creature. He subjected it to interrogation under torture, filed the written record of the inquest, examined it, passed sentence, and reported the outcome. He produced the rat and the meat as evidence, closed the entire case, and had the convict torn apart alive in the courtyard.

sentences.zh: 父見之，視文辭如老獄吏，大驚，遂使書獄。

translations.literal: The father saw it, looked at the phrasing as that of an old jail clerk, was greatly startled, and thereupon had him write cases.
translations.idiomatic: When his father read the document, the wording read like that of a veteran prison clerk. He was astonished and thereafter set the boy to drafting legal briefs.

sentences.zh: 父死後，湯為長安吏。

translations.literal: After the father died, Tang became a clerk of Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: After his father died, Tang took a minor post as a clerk in Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 周陽侯為諸卿時，嘗系長安，湯傾身事之。

translations.literal: When the Marquis of Zhouyang was among the various ministers, he had once been imprisoned in Chang'an; Tang devoted himself entirely to serving him.
translations.idiomatic: The Marquis of Zhouyang had once been held in custody in Chang'an while he was still one of the nine ministers; Tang threw himself into helping him in every way he could.

sentences.zh: 及出為侯，大與湯交，遍見貴人。

translations.literal: When he came out and became a marquis, he greatly associated with Tang and introduced him everywhere among the powerful.
translations.idiomatic: Once the marquis was free and ennobled, he became Tang's steadfast patron and presented him to everyone who mattered at court.

sentences.zh: 湯給事內史，為甯成掾，以湯為無害，言大府，調茂陵尉，治方中。

translations.literal: Tang served in the office of the metropolitan superintendent, as an aide to Ning Cheng; because Tang was considered harmless, he was mentioned to the great office, transferred to be captain of Maoling, and supervised the imperial mausoleum precinct.
translations.idiomatic: Tang was assigned to the metropolitan governor's staff as an aide to Ning Cheng. Deemed reliable, he was recommended to the capital authorities, transferred to the post of captain of Maoling, and put in charge of work within the tomb park.

sentences.zh: 武安侯為丞相，徵湯為史，薦補侍御史。

translations.literal: When the Marquis of Wu'an became chancellor, he summoned Tang as a clerk and recommended him to fill a vacancy as attendant censor.
translations.idiomatic: When the Marquis of Wu'an became chancellor, he called Tang up as a clerk on his staff and had him promoted to attendant censor.

sentences.zh: 治陳皇后巫蠱獄，深竟黨與，上以為能，遷太史大夫。

translations.literal: In handling the case of witchcraft against Empress Chen, he pursued the clique to the utmost; the emperor thought him capable and promoted him to grand clerk.
translations.idiomatic: He prosecuted the witchcraft case involving Empress Chen, tracking every accomplice to the end. The emperor judged him competent and raised him to grand clerk of records.

sentences.zh: 與趙禹共定諸律令，務在深文，拘守職之吏。

translations.literal: Together with Zhao Yu he fixed the various statutes and ordinances, striving for harsh wording and restraining clerks who guarded their duties.
translations.idiomatic: He and Zhao Yu rewrote the statutes and regulations, aiming for severe, tight legal language that would keep petty officials from shirking their responsibilities.

sentences.zh: 已而禹至少府，湯為廷尉，兩人交歡，兄事禹。

translations.literal: Afterward Yu reached the chamberlain for the palace revenues; Tang became commandant of justice. The two were on warm terms, and Tang treated Yu as an elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yu later rose to chamberlain for the palace revenues while Tang became commandant of justice. The two remained on excellent terms, and Tang deferred to Yu as he would to an older brother.

sentences.zh: 禹志在奉公孤立，而湯舞知以御人。

translations.literal: Yu's intent was to uphold public duty in isolation, while Tang wielded cleverness to control others.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yu set his heart on impartial public service and stood aloof from intrigue; Tang, by contrast, traded on his wits to manipulate those around him.

sentences.zh: 始為小吏，干沒，與長安富賈田甲、魚翁叔之屬交私。

translations.literal: When he first was a petty clerk, he embezzled and associated privately with Chang'an rich merchants such as Tian Jia and Yu Wengshu.
translations.idiomatic: In his early days as a low clerk he skimmed funds and kept company with wealthy Chang'an traders like Tian Jia and Yu Wengshu.

sentences.zh: 及列九卿，收接天下名士大夫，己內心雖不合，然陽浮道與之。

translations.literal: When he reached the nine ministers, he received the famous gentlemen of the empire; though inwardly he did not agree with them, he outwardly floated the Way with them.
translations.idiomatic: Once he sat among the nine ministers, he cultivated ties with eminent scholars from every quarter. Inwardly he often disagreed with them, yet he publicly affected the rhetoric of classical learning.

sentences.zh: 是時，上方鄉文學，湯決大獄，欲傅古義，乃請博士弟子治《尚書》、《春秋》，補廷尉史，平亭疑法。

translations.literal: At this time the emperor was inclining toward literary learning; Tang decided great cases and wished to attach ancient meanings, and therefore requested students of the erudits versed in the Documents and the Spring and Autumn to supplement as clerks of the commandant of justice and settle doubtful points of law.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was then turning toward Confucian scholarship. When Tang tried major cases he liked to cloak his rulings in classical precedent, so he asked the court academicians to assign disciples trained in the Documents and the Spring and Autumn to serve as clerks in his office and help iron out disputed interpretations of the law.

sentences.zh: 奏讞疑，必奏先為上分別其原，上所是，受而著讞法廷尉挈令，揚主之明。

translations.literal: When memorializing doubtful judgments, he always first memorialized to distinguish the origins for the ruler; what the ruler approved, he received and set down as precedent in the commandant's binding rules, displaying the ruler's brilliance.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever he submitted a doubtful case for decision, he first laid out the legal reasoning on both sides for the throne. Whatever the emperor endorsed, he incorporated into the commandant's codified precedents, giving full credit to the sovereign's discernment.

sentences.zh: 奏事即譴，湯摧謝，鄉上意所便，必引正監掾史賢者，曰：「固為臣議，如上責臣，臣弗用，愚抵此。」

translations.literal: When on submitting a matter he was immediately rebuked, Tang yielded and apologized, inclining toward what suited the ruler's mind, and always cited the upright supervisor and clerk worthies, saying, "They certainly deliberated for your servant; as your Majesty blames your servant, your servant failed to adopt it—stupidity brought me to this."
translations.idiomatic: If the emperor faulted a memorial as soon as it was presented, Tang at once withdrew it with an apology and bent to the sovereign's wishes. He would then name some worthy among his supervisors or clerks and say, "These men urged that course on me; when Your Majesty censures me, it is because I was too dull to follow their advice."

sentences.zh: 罪常釋。

translations.literal: The fault was always released.
translations.idiomatic: The blame thus rarely stuck to him.

sentences.zh: 間即奏事，上善之，曰：「臣非知為此奏，乃監、掾、史某所為。」

translations.literal: Sometimes when he submitted a matter and the ruler praised it, he said, "Your servant did not know to make this memorial—it was done by supervisor and clerk so-and-so."
translations.idiomatic: When a proposal won praise, he would say, "I cannot claim credit for that memorial—it was drafted by such-and-such a supervisor or clerk."

sentences.zh: 其欲薦吏，揚人之善、解人之過如此。

translations.literal: In wishing to recommend clerks, he thus praised people's good points and excused people's faults.
translations.idiomatic: That was how he promoted subordinates: he broadcast their merits and glossed over their lapses.

sentences.zh: 所治即上意所欲罪，予監吏深刻者；

translations.literal: In what he handled, if it was what the ruler's mind wished to condemn, he gave it to a supervisor clerk who was harsh and deep;
translations.idiomatic: If the emperor wanted a man ruined, Tang handed the file to a clerk known for pitiless severity.

sentences.zh: 即上意所欲釋，予監吏輕平者。

translations.literal: If it was what the ruler's mind wished to release, he gave it to a supervisor clerk who was lenient and even.
translations.idiomatic: If the emperor wished someone spared, he assigned the case to a clerk with a reputation for leniency and even-handedness.

sentences.zh: 所治即豪，必舞文巧詆；

translations.literal: In what he handled, if it was a powerful house, he always twisted the text and craftily slandered;
translations.idiomatic: Against powerful families he would twist the statute book and frame charges with malicious ingenuity.

sentences.zh: 即下戶羸弱，時口言「雖文致法，上裁察。」

translations.literal: If it was a low household, weak and feeble, he sometimes orally said, "Although the text reaches the law, let the ruler examine and decide."
translations.idiomatic: Against humble, defenseless commoners he would sometimes remark aloud, "The letter of the law may condemn him, but let His Majesty weigh the matter."

sentences.zh: 於是往往釋湯所言。

translations.literal: Thereupon often what Tang said was released.
translations.idiomatic: Time and again the court accepted Tang's wording and let the accused go.

sentences.zh: 湯至於大吏，內行修，交通賓客飲食，於故人子弟為吏及貧昆弟，調護之尤厚，其造請諸公，不避寒暑。

translations.literal: Tang, reaching great clerk, cultivated inner conduct, associated with guests for drink and food, and toward old friends' sons who were clerks and poor brothers, regulated and protected them especially thickly; in visiting the great lords he did not avoid cold or heat.
translations.idiomatic: Though he had risen to high office, he kept up scrupulous private conduct, entertained a wide circle, and looked after the sons of old friends who held petty posts and his own indigent kinsmen with exceptional generosity. He called on the great ministers in person, whatever the season.

sentences.zh: 是以湯雖文深意忌不專平，然得此聲譽。

translations.literal: Therefore although Tang, though the text was deep and his mind jealous, was not solely even, yet he obtained this reputation.
translations.idiomatic: So despite legal harshness and a jealous temperament—far from even-handed—he enjoyed a reputation for benevolence.

sentences.zh: 而深刻吏多為爪牙用者，依於文學之士。

translations.literal: And the many harsh deep clerks who were used as claws and fangs relied on men of literary learning.
translations.idiomatic: The ruthless clerks who served as his enforcers likewise wrapped themselves in the language of the classics.

sentences.zh: 丞相弘數稱其美。

translations.literal: Chancellor Gongsun Hong repeatedly praised his excellence.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Gongsun Hong often spoke of him in glowing terms.

sentences.zh: 及治淮南、衡山、江都反獄，皆窮根本。

translations.literal: When he handled the rebellion cases of Huainan, Hengshan, and Jiangdu, all were exhausted to the root.
translations.idiomatic: In the treason trials of Huainan, Hengshan, and Jiangdu he pursued every lead to the bottom.

sentences.zh: 嚴助、伍被，上欲釋之，湯爭曰：「伍被本造反謀，而助親幸出入禁闥，腹心之臣，乃交私諸侯如此，弗誅，後不可治。」

translations.literal: Yan Zhu and Wu Bei—the ruler wished to release them; Tang contended, saying, "Wu Bei at root plotted rebellion, and Zhu, personally favored, going in and out of the inner palace, a heart-and-belly minister, yet privately associated with the feudal lords like this—if not executed, later cannot be governed."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor wanted to pardon Yan Zhu and Wu Bei. Tang objected: "Wu Bei helped lay the plot from the beginning, and Yan Zhu enjoyed intimate access to the inner apartments—he was a minister of the ruler's inmost counsels—yet he secretly conspired with the kings. If they are not put to death, the law will never again command respect."

sentences.zh: 上可論之。

translations.literal: The ruler approved the judgment.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted his argument and condemned them.

sentences.zh: 其治獄所巧排大臣自以為功，多此類。

translations.literal: In handling cases, the clever way he displaced great ministers, taking credit for himself, was mostly of this kind.
translations.idiomatic: His habit of outmaneuvering senior ministers and claiming the credit in such trials was typical of his style.

sentences.zh: 繇是益尊任，遷御史大夫。

translations.literal: From this he was increasingly honored and employed, and promoted to imperial counselor.
translations.idiomatic: He was therefore trusted more heavily and promoted to imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 會渾邪等降，漢大興兵伐匈奴，山東水旱，貧民流徙，皆卬給縣官，縣官空虛。

translations.literal: It happened that Hunye and others surrendered; Han greatly raised troops to attack the Xiongnu; east of the mountains flood and drought; poor people wandered and migrated—all looked to the government for supplies, and the government treasuries were empty.
translations.idiomatic: When Hunye and his followers submitted, the Han mobilized on a vast scale against the Xiongnu; floods and drought struck the eastern heartland; refugees streamed from their homes and looked to the state for relief until the public granaries stood bare.

sentences.zh: 湯承上指，請造白金及五銖錢，籠天下鹽鐵，排富商大賈，出告緡令，鋤豪強並兼之家，舞文巧詆以輔法。

translations.literal: Tang received the ruler's intent, requested to mint white metal and five-zhu cash, monopolize salt and iron over the empire, displace great rich merchants, issued the denunciation ordinance, rooted out powerful families who swallowed others' holdings, and twisted text and craftily slandered to assist the law.
translations.idiomatic: Reading the emperor's wishes, Tang proposed minting the white-metal currency and the five-zhu coin, bringing the empire's salt and iron under state monopoly, squeezing the great merchant houses, promulgating the "report-your-neighbor's-wealth" statute, and breaking up powerful clans that engrossed land and labor—all the while bending the statutes to give the policy a legal veneer.

sentences.zh: 湯每朝奏事，語國家用，日旰，天子忘食。

translations.literal: Tang each time at court submitted affairs, discoursing on state use, until the sun was late; the Son of Heaven forgot food.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Tang spoke at audience on fiscal policy, he went on until the sun stood low in the west, and the Son of Heaven forgot his meal.

sentences.zh: 丞相取充位，天子事皆決湯。

translations.literal: The chancellor held a filling seat; the Son of Heaven's affairs were all decided by Tang.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor was a figurehead; every important decision passed through Tang.

sentences.zh: 百姓不安其生，騷動，縣官所興未獲其利，奸吏並侵漁，於是痛繩以罪。

translations.literal: The hundred surnames were not secure in their lives, stirred and restless; what the government raised did not obtain its profit, and wicked clerks together encroached and preyed—thereupon he painfully bound them with guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Common folk could not live in peace; unrest spread. State ventures brought little gain while corrupt clerks fed on the people, so Tang cracked down with harsh punishments.

sentences.zh: 自公卿以下至於庶人咸指湯。

translations.literal: From the dukes and ministers down to the common people, all pointed at Tang.
translations.idiomatic: From the highest ministers to the meanest commoner, everyone blamed Tang.

sentences.zh: 湯嘗病，上自至捨視，其隆貴如此。

translations.literal: Tang once was ill; the ruler in person came to his lodging to look—his exalted honor was thus.
translations.idiomatic: When Tang fell ill, the emperor visited his house in person—such was the height of his favor.

sentences.zh: 匈奴求和親，群臣議前，博士狄山曰：「和親便。」

translations.literal: The Xiongnu sought peace and kinship; the various ministers deliberated before the court; Erudite Di Shan said, "Peace and kinship is convenient."
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu sued for a marriage alliance. At the council before the throne, Academician Di Shan said, "An alliance by marriage is the prudent course."

sentences.zh: 上問其便，山曰：「兵，凶器，未易數動。

translations.literal: The ruler asked wherein lay the convenience; Shan said, "Weapons are ill-omened tools; it is not easy to move them repeatedly.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked why. Shan replied, "Arms are unlucky instruments; they should not be lightly wielded again and again.

sentences.zh: 高帝欲伐匈奴，大困平城，乃遂結和親。

translations.literal: The High Emperor wished to attack the Xiongnu, was greatly distressed at Pingcheng, and thereupon concluded peace and kinship.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu tried to strike the Xiongnu and was trapped in desperate straits at Pingcheng; after that he settled for peace through intermarriage."

sentences.zh: 孝惠、高后時，天下安樂，及文帝欲事匈奴，北邊蕭然苦兵。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaohui and Empress Gao, the empire was peaceful and happy; when Emperor Wen wished to serve the Xiongnu, the northern border was desolate and bitter with soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Hui and Empress Lü the realm knew quiet prosperity. When Emperor Wen turned against the Xiongnu, the northern frontier knew nothing but the miseries of war.

sentences.zh: 孝景時，吳、楚七國反，景帝往來東宮間，天下寒心數月。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaojing, Wu and Chu and the seven states rebelled; Emperor Jing went back and forth between the eastern palaces; the empire was cold in heart for months.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Jing the seven kingdoms of Wu and Chu rose in revolt; the emperor shuttled between the two palace complexes while the whole country held its breath for months on end.

sentences.zh: 吳、楚已破，竟景帝不言兵，天下富實。

translations.literal: Wu and Chu already broken, to the end of Emperor Jing he did not speak of soldiers; the empire was rich and solid.
translations.idiomatic: After Wu and Chu were crushed, Jing never again spoke of major campaigns, and the realm grew wealthy and secure.

sentences.zh: 今自陛下興兵擊匈奴，中國以空虛，邊大困貧。

translations.literal: Now from Your Majesty raising troops to strike the Xiongnu, the central states thereby are empty; the border is greatly distressed and poor.
translations.idiomatic: Since Your Majesty took the field against the Xiongnu, the interior has been drained and the frontier left exhausted and destitute.

sentences.zh: 由是觀之，不如和親。」

translations.literal: From this viewing it, it is not as good as peace and kinship."
translations.idiomatic: Judged by that record, an alliance is still the better policy."

sentences.zh: 上問湯，湯曰：「此愚儒無知。」

translations.literal: The ruler asked Tang; Tang said, "This is a foolish Ru who knows nothing."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor turned to Tang, who said, "That is the talk of a pedantic scholar who understands nothing."

sentences.zh: 狄山曰：「臣固愚忠，若御史大夫湯，乃詐忠。

translations.literal: Di Shan said, "Your servant is indeed foolishly loyal; as for Imperial Counselor Tang, he is feignedly loyal.
translations.idiomatic: Di Shan retorted, "I may be a simple loyalist, but your imperial counselor is loyal only in pretense.

sentences.zh: 湯之治淮南、江都，以深文痛詆諸侯，別疏骨肉，使籓臣不自安，臣固知湯之詐忠。」

translations.literal: Tang's handling of Huainan and Jiangdu, with deep text painfully slandering the feudal lords, separating and estranging bone and flesh, caused border ministers not to be secure in themselves—your servant indeed knows Tang's feigned loyalty."
translations.idiomatic: In the Huainan and Jiangdu trials he stretched the law to vilify the kings, pitting kin against kin until no prince felt safe at his post. That is the loyalty I call false."

sentences.zh: 於是上作色曰：「吾使生居一郡，能無使虜入盜乎？」

translations.literal: Thereupon the ruler changed color and said, "I send you, sir, to dwell in one commandery—can you prevent the barbarians from entering and stealing?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor's face darkened. "I give you one commandery," he said. "Could you keep the raiders out?"

sentences.zh: 山曰：「不能。」

translations.literal: Shan said, "I cannot."
translations.idiomatic: "No," said Shan.

sentences.zh: 曰：「居一縣？」

translations.literal: He said, "Dwell in one county?"
translations.idiomatic: "What about one county?"

sentences.zh: 曰：「不能。」

translations.literal: He said, "I cannot."
translations.idiomatic: "I could not," Shan admitted.

sentences.zh: 復曰：「居一鄣間？」

translations.literal: Again he said, "Dwell within one barrier interval?"
translations.idiomatic: "Very well—one fortified barrier sector along the wall?"

sentences.zh: 山自度辯窮且下吏，曰：「能。」

translations.literal: Shan himself reckoned his argument exhausted and about to go down to clerks, and said, "I can."
translations.idiomatic: Shan saw he had argued himself into a corner and would soon be handed over to the law; he answered, "I could."

sentences.zh: 乃譴山乘鄣。

translations.literal: Thereupon he sent Shan to ride the barrier.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor promptly sent him to hold a signal tower on the frontier.

sentences.zh: 至月餘，匈奴斬山頭而去。

translations.literal: After more than a month, the Xiongnu cut off Shan's head and left.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a month later the Xiongnu rode up and took his head.

sentences.zh: 是後群臣震讋。

translations.literal: After this the various ministers were shaken and fearful.
translations.idiomatic: After that no minister dared speak lightly against the war party.

sentences.zh: 湯客田甲雖賈人，有賢操，始湯為小吏，與錢通，及為大吏，而甲所以責湯行義，有烈士之風。

translations.literal: Tang's guest Tian Jia, although a merchant, had worthy conduct; when Tang was a petty clerk, he had intercourse with money with him; when he became a great clerk, what Jia used to reprove Tang's conduct had the air of a man of fierce integrity.
translations.idiomatic: Tang's client Tian Jia was a tradesman, yet he lived by a stern code of honor. Tang had borrowed money from him in his clerk days; after Tang rose high, Jia still rebuked his conduct like a man ready to die for principle.

sentences.zh: 湯為御史大夫七歲，敗。

translations.literal: Tang was imperial counselor seven years, and was ruined.
translations.idiomatic: Seven years as imperial counselor ended in his downfall.

sentences.zh: 河東人李文，故嘗與湯有隙，已而為御史中丞，薦數從中文事有可以傷湯者，不能為地。

translations.literal: Li Wen of Hedong had formerly had a rift with Tang; afterward he became vice director of the secretariat and repeatedly from central documents had matters that could injure Tang; he could not make room.
translations.idiomatic: Li Wen of Hedong had long been Tang's enemy. When Wen became vice director of the imperial secretariat, he combed the palace files for anything that might incriminate Tang and showed him no quarter.

sentences.zh: 湯有所愛史魯謁居，知湯弗平，使人上飛變告文奸事，事下湯，湯治論殺文，而湯心知謁居為之。

translations.literal: Tang had a favored clerk Lu Yéju; knowing Tang was not reconciled, he had someone submit a flying denunciation reporting Wen's wicked affairs; the matter went down to Tang; Tang handled it and judged and killed Wen, while Tang inwardly knew Yéju had done it.
translations.idiomatic: Tang kept a favorite clerk, Lu Yéju, who saw how Tang brooded on the grudge. Yéju arranged for a sealed denunciation accusing Wen of misconduct; when the case landed in Tang's hands, he tried Wen and had him executed, all the while knowing Yéju was the author of the charge.

sentences.zh: 上問：「變事從跡安起？」

translations.literal: The ruler asked, "Whence arose the traces of the denunciation affair?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked, "Who is behind this anonymous accusation?"

sentences.zh: 湯陽驚曰：「此殆文故人怨之。」

translations.literal: Tang feigned surprise and said, "This is probably an old friend of Wen who resents him."
translations.idiomatic: Tang put on a show of surprise. "Almost certainly some old enemy of Li Wen," he said.

sentences.zh: 謁居病臥閭里主人，湯自往視病，為謁居摩足，趙國以冶鑄為業，王數訟鐵官事，湯常排趙王。

translations.literal: Yéju was ill and lay at his ward host's; Tang in person went to look at the illness and for Yéju rubbed feet; Zhao state took smelting founding as occupation; the king repeatedly sued iron office affairs; Tang often displaced the King of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: When Yéju fell ill and lodged with a neighbor, Tang visited him in person and massaged his feet. The kingdom of Zhao lived by its foundries; its king repeatedly sued the monopoly officials, and Tang as a rule ruled against the king.

sentences.zh: 趙王求湯陰事。

translations.literal: The King of Zhao sought Tang's secret affairs.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Zhao set out to dig up dirt on Tang.

sentences.zh: 謁居嘗案趙王，趙王怨之，並上書告：「湯大臣也，史謁居有病，湯至為摩足，疑與為大奸。」

translations.literal: Yéju had once investigated the King of Zhao; the king resented him, and together submitted a memorial reporting, "Tang is a great minister; clerk Yéju was ill, yet Tang went so far as to rub his feet—suspicion is that together they commit great wickedness."
translations.idiomatic: Yéju had once prosecuted the king, who nursed a grudge. The king now sent up a joint memorial: "Zhang Tang is a chief minister; when his clerk Yéju fell ill, Tang actually massaged the man's feet—clear evidence of some monstrous conspiracy between them."

sentences.zh: 事下延尉。

translations.literal: The matter went down to the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: The case was referred to the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 謁居病死，事連其弟，弟系導官。

translations.literal: Yéju died of illness; the affair connected his younger brother; the younger brother was imprisoned at the guide office.
translations.idiomatic: Yéju died before trial, but the investigation ensnared his younger brother, who was locked up in the guide office prison.

sentences.zh: 湯亦治它囚導官，見謁居弟，欲陰為之，而陽不省。

translations.literal: Tang also handled other prisoners at the guide office; seeing Yéju's younger brother, he wished secretly to favor him, but outwardly feigned not to recognize.
translations.idiomatic: Tang happened to be interrogating other inmates there. When he saw Yéju's brother, he meant to help him quietly while pretending not to notice him.

sentences.zh: 謁居弟不知而怨湯，使人上書，告湯與謁居謀，共變李文。

translations.literal: Yéju's younger brother did not know and resented Tang, and had someone submit a memorial reporting Tang and Yéju plotted together and together changed Li Wen.
translations.idiomatic: The brother did not understand and blamed Tang; he had someone memorialize that Tang and Yéju had conspired to frame Li Wen.

sentences.zh: 事下減宣。

translations.literal: The matter went down to Jian Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: The case was assigned to Jian Xuan.

sentences.zh: 宣嘗與湯有隙，及得此事，窮竟其事，未奏也。

translations.literal: Xuan had once had a rift with Tang; when he obtained this affair, he pursued it to the utmost; he had not yet memorialized.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan had old scores with Tang; he seized the chance to investigate to the limit, though he had not yet reported his findings.

sentences.zh: 會人有盜發孝文園瘞錢，丞相青翟朝，與湯約俱謝，至前，湯念獨丞相以四時行園，當謝，湯無與也，不謝。

translations.literal: It happened that someone dug up and stole the buried cash of Emperor Xiaowen's park; Chancellor Qingzhai at court, with Tang agreed together to apologize; arriving before the ruler, Tang thought only the chancellor by the four seasons went to the park and ought to apologize; Tang had no part in it and did not apologize.
translations.idiomatic: Then robbers broke into the burial mound in Emperor Wen's mausoleum park and stole the consecrated coins. At court Chancellor Zhuang Qingzhai agreed with Tang that they would both apologize. When they came before the throne, Tang reflected that only the chancellor made the seasonal rounds of the shrines and therefore bore responsibility; he himself had nothing to do with it, so he offered no apology.

sentences.zh: 丞相謝，上使御史案其事。

translations.literal: The chancellor apologized; the ruler ordered the censor to investigate the affair.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor apologized alone, and the emperor ordered the censorate to look into the matter.

sentences.zh: 湯欲致其文丞相見知，丞相患之。

translations.literal: Tang wished to bring his documents so the chancellor would be shown to know; the chancellor worried.
translations.idiomatic: Tang meant to shape the documentary evidence so as to implicate the chancellor in prior knowledge; the chancellor grew alarmed.

sentences.zh: 三長史皆害湯，欲陷之。

translations.literal: The three chief clerks all harmed Tang and wished to trap him.
translations.idiomatic: The three chief clerks of the chancellor's office hated Tang and conspired to destroy him.

sentences.zh: 始，長史硃買臣素怨湯，語在其傳。

translations.literal: At first Chief Clerk Zhu Maichen had long resented Tang—the words are in his biography.
translations.idiomatic: Chief Clerk Zhu Maichen had nursed a grudge against Tang for years—the story is told in his own chapter.

sentences.zh: 王朝，齊人，以術至右內史。

translations.literal: Wang Chao, a man of Qi, by arts reached right metropolitan superintendent.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chao of Qi had risen to right metropolitan superintendent through his technical expertise.

sentences.zh: 邊通學短長，剛暴人也。

translations.literal: Bian Tong studied short and long; he was a violent fierce man.
translations.idiomatic: Bian Tong was a specialist in the "short and long" school of persuasion—abrasive, violent-tempered.

sentences.zh: 官至濟南相。

translations.literal: Official rank reached Jinan chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: He had served as chancellor of the kingdom of Jinan.

sentences.zh: 故皆居湯右，已而失官，守長史，詘體於湯。

translations.literal: Therefore all had been to Tang's right; afterward they lost office, guarded as chief clerks, and bent their bodies before Tang.
translations.idiomatic: All three had once outranked Tang; later they were stripped of their posts and ended up as chief clerks under him, forced to humble themselves.

sentences.zh: 湯數行丞相事，知此三長史素貴，常陵折之。

translations.literal: Tang repeatedly acted chancellor affairs, knew these three chief clerks had been noble, and always humiliated and broke them.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Tang acted for the chancellor, he knew how exalted these three had been and took every chance to humiliate them.

sentences.zh: 故三長史合謀曰：「始湯約與君謝，已而賣君；

translations.literal: Therefore the three chief clerks conspired and said, "At first Tang agreed with you, sir, to apologize together, afterward selling you;
translations.idiomatic: The three chief clerks put their heads together. "At first Zhang Tang pledged to apologize with you," they said, "then he betrayed you;

sentences.zh: 今欲劾君以宗廟事，此欲代君耳。

translations.literal: Now he wishes to impeach you, sir, on the ancestral temple affair—this is because he wishes to replace you, sir.
translations.idiomatic: now he means to impeach you over the desecration of the imperial tombs because he wants your seat;

sentences.zh: 吾知湯陰事。」

translations.literal: We know Tang's secret affairs."
translations.idiomatic: and we know the dirty secrets he is hiding."

sentences.zh: 使吏捕案湯左田信等，曰湯且欲為請奏，信輒先知之，居物致富，與湯分之。

translations.literal: He had clerks arrest and investigate Tang's confidant Tian Xin and others, saying Tang was about to memorialize for approval, Xin always knew beforehand, hoarded goods and grew rich, and divided with Tang.
translations.idiomatic: They had agents seize Tang's confederate Tian Xin, charging that whenever Tang was about to memorialize a ruling, Xin learned of it in advance, cornered goods, grew rich, and split the profits with Tang.

sentences.zh: 及它奸事。

translations.literal: And other wicked affairs.
translations.idiomatic: They added further counts of misconduct.

sentences.zh: 事辭頗聞。

translations.literal: The affair's words were somewhat heard.
translations.idiomatic: Word of the accusations began to reach the throne.

sentences.zh: 上問湯曰：「吾所為，賈人輒知，益居其物，是類有以吾謀告之者。」

translations.literal: The ruler asked Tang, saying, "What I do, merchants always know and increase hoarding their goods—is there someone of this kind who tells them my plans?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked Tang, "Whatever policy I mean to adopt, the merchants seem to know it in advance and stockpile accordingly. Someone must be leaking my deliberations."

sentences.zh: 湯不謝，又陽驚曰：「固宜有。」

translations.literal: Tang did not apologize, and again feigned surprise and said, "Indeed there ought to be."
translations.idiomatic: Tang offered no denial and again feigned surprise: "No doubt there is."

sentences.zh: 減宜亦奏謁居事。

translations.literal: Jian Xuan also memorialized the Yéju affair.
translations.idiomatic: Jian Xuan weighed in with his own memorial on the Yéju business.

sentences.zh: 上以湯懷詐面欺，使使八輩簿責湯。

translations.literal: The ruler thought Tang harbored deceit and faced and deceived him, and sent envoys eight times to register-blame Tang.
translations.idiomatic: Convinced that Tang had lied to his face, the emperor dispatched eight successive envoys to cross-examine him from the written record.

sentences.zh: 湯具自道無此，不服。

translations.literal: Tang fully himself said there was not this and did not submit.
translations.idiomatic: Tang insisted he was innocent and refused to confess.

sentences.zh: 於是上使趙禹責湯。

translations.literal: Thereupon the ruler sent Zhao Yu to blame Tang.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then sent Zhao Yu to confront him.

sentences.zh: 禹至，讓湯曰：「君何不知分也！

translations.literal: When Yu arrived, he reproached Tang, saying, "Sir, how do you not know your lot!
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao Yu arrived, he rebuked Tang. "Have you no sense of proportion?

sentences.zh: 君所治，夷滅者幾何人矣！

translations.literal: What you have handled—how many people have been exterminated and extinguished!
translations.idiomatic: Think how many families you have destroyed in the cases you have tried!

sentences.zh: 今人言君皆有狀，天子重致君獄，欲令君自為計，何多以對為？」

translations.literal: Now men who speak of you all have substance; the Son of Heaven gravely sends you to prison, wishing to make you, sir, devise your own course—why multiply answers?"
translations.idiomatic: Every charge against you now carries weight. His Majesty is moving to commit you to prison so you may settle your own affairs—why prolong the interrogation with endless denials?"

sentences.zh: 湯乃為書謝曰：「湯無尺寸之功，起刀筆吏，陛下幸致位三公，無以塞責。

translations.literal: Tang thereupon made a document apologizing, saying, "Tang has not an inch of merit; he rose from knife-and-brush clerk; Your Majesty graciously raised him to the three dukes—there is no way to answer for his duty.
translations.idiomatic: Tang then drafted a memorial of apology: "I began as a mere scribal clerk and owe my rise to the three highest offices entirely to Your Majesty's favor—yet I have failed in my responsibility.

sentences.zh: 然謀陷湯者，三長史也。」

translations.literal: Yet those who plotted to trap Tang are the three chief clerks."
translations.idiomatic: Those who schemed to bring me down are the three chief clerks of the chancellor's office."

sentences.zh: 遂自殺。

translations.literal: Thereupon he killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: He then took his own life.

sentences.zh: 湯死，家產直不過五百金，皆所得奉賜，無它贏。

translations.literal: When Tang died, his household property was worth no more than five hundred catties of gold, all from salaries and gifts received, no other surplus.
translations.idiomatic: At his death his estate amounted to no more than five hundred catties of gold, every ounce of it from official salary and imperial gifts—nothing ill-gotten.

sentences.zh: 昆弟諸子欲厚葬湯，湯母曰：「湯為天子大臣，被惡言而死，何厚葬為！」

translations.literal: Brothers and various sons wished to bury Tang richly; Tang's mother said, "Tang was a great minister of the Son of Heaven; he died bearing evil words—what is rich burial for!"
translations.idiomatic: His brothers and sons wanted an elaborate funeral. His mother said, "He was a chief minister of the throne and died under a cloud of slander—what good would pomp do?"

sentences.zh: 載以牛車，有棺而無槨。

translations.literal: They carried him on an ox cart, having a coffin but no outer coffin.
translations.idiomatic: He was taken to the grave on an ox cart, in a plain inner coffin with no outer shell.

sentences.zh: 上聞之，曰：「非此母不生此子。」

translations.literal: The ruler heard it and said, "Were it not for this mother, such a son would not have been born."
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor heard, he remarked, "Only such a mother could have raised such a son."

sentences.zh: 乃盡按誅三長史。

translations.literal: Thereupon he fully investigated and executed the three chief clerks.
translations.idiomatic: He then had all three chief clerks arrested, tried, and executed.

sentences.zh: 丞相青翟自殺。

translations.literal: Chancellor Qingzhai killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Zhuang Qingzhai committed suicide.

sentences.zh: 出田信。

translations.literal: Released Tian Xin.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Xin was cleared and released.

sentences.zh: 上惜湯，復稍進其子安世。

translations.literal: The ruler pitied Tang and again gradually promoted his son Anshi.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor still regretted Tang's fate and began again to advance his son Zhang Anshi.

sentences.zh: 子 安世

translations.literal: Son: Anshi.
translations.idiomatic: His son: Zhang Anshi.

sentences.zh: 安世字子孺，少以父任為郎。

translations.literal: Anshi, courtesy Ziru, in youth by his father's privilege became gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Anshi, courtesy name Ziru, entered the court in his youth as a gentleman-attendant by virtue of his father's rank.

sentences.zh: 用善書給事尚書，精力於職，休沐未嘗出。

translations.literal: By good writing he served in the Masters of Writing, exerting energy on the duty; on rest days he never went out.
translations.idiomatic: His neat calligraphy won him a post with the Masters of Writing. He threw himself into the work and never left the capital even on his statutory days off.

sentences.zh: 上行幸河東，嘗亡書三篋，詔問莫能知，唯安世識之，具作其事。

translations.literal: The ruler traveled to Hedong; once three chests of documents were lost; an edict asked and none could know; only Anshi recognized them and fully wrote out their contents.
translations.idiomatic: On an imperial progress to Hedong three document cases went missing. An edict summoned anyone who could identify them; only Anshi recognized the files from memory and wrote out their contents in full.

sentences.zh: 後購求得書，以相校無所遺失。

translations.literal: Afterward by purchase they sought and obtained the documents; comparing them there was nothing missing or lost.
translations.idiomatic: When the originals were later recovered and checked, nothing was missing.

sentences.zh: 上奇其材，擢為尚書令，遷光祿大夫。

translations.literal: The ruler marveled at his talent, promoted him to director of the Masters of Writing, and transferred him to grand coachman.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was so impressed that he promoted him to director of the Masters of Writing, then to grand coachman of the household.

sentences.zh: 時，霍光子禹為右將軍，上亦以禹為大司馬，罷其右將軍屯兵，以虛尊加之，而實奪其眾。

translations.literal: At this time Huo Guang's son Yu was general of the right; the ruler also made Yu grand marshal, abolished his right general garrison troops, applied empty honor to him, and in reality stripped his hosts.
translations.idiomatic: At that time Huo Guang's son Huo Yu held the post of general of the right while also bearing the title of grand marshal. The emperor stripped him of his field command, leaving the grand marshal's title a hollow honor while removing his real military following.

sentences.zh: 後歲餘，禹謀反，夷宗族，安世素小心畏忌，已內憂矣。

translations.literal: After more than a year, Yu plotted rebellion; the clan was exterminated; Anshi had always been careful and fearful—already he was inwardly worried.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a year later Huo Yu rose in rebellion and his entire clan was wiped out. Anshi, who had always been cautious and easily alarmed, was already anxious at heart.

sentences.zh: 其女孫敬為霍氏外屬婦，當相坐，安世瘦懼，形於顏色，上怪而憐之，以問左右，乃赦敬，以尉其意。

translations.literal: His granddaughter Jing was wife of a collateral of the Huo clan and ought to suffer connected guilt; Anshi was emaciated with fear, showing in face color; the ruler wondered and pitied him, asked those at his side, and thereupon pardoned Jing, in order to comfort his mind.
translations.idiomatic: His granddaughter Zhang Jing had married into a Huo-affiliated family and stood liable to collective punishment. Anshi grew haggard with dread, and it showed on his face. The emperor noticed, took pity, asked his attendants for the story, and pardoned Jing to set his mind at ease.

sentences.zh: 安世浸恐。

translations.literal: Anshi gradually was more fearful.
translations.idiomatic: His fear only deepened.

sentences.zh: 職典樞機，以謹慎周密自著，外內無間。

translations.literal: He held office governing the pivot machinery, and by careful cautious thoroughness made himself known; outside and inside there was no gap.
translations.idiomatic: He held the levers of confidential policy, winning a reputation for meticulous care. Not a whisper of what passed in the inner palace reached the outer court through him, nor vice versa.

sentences.zh: 每定大政，已決，輒移病出；

translations.literal: Each time a great policy was settled, already decided, he always shifted illness and went out;
translations.idiomatic: Whenever a major decision had been taken, he would plead illness and withdraw;

sentences.zh: 聞有詔令，乃驚，使吏之丞相府問焉。

translations.literal: hearing there was an edict order, then was startled, and sent a clerk to the chancellor's office to ask.
translations.idiomatic: and if he then heard that an edict had been issued, he would start in alarm and dispatch a clerk to the chancellor's office to learn what it said.

sentences.zh: 自朝廷大臣莫知其與議也。

translations.literal: From the court great ministers none knew his participation in deliberation.
translations.idiomatic: Even senior ministers at court had no idea that he had been in on the discussion.

sentences.zh: 嘗有所薦，其人來謝，安世大恨，以為舉賢達能，豈有私謝邪？

translations.literal: Once he had recommended someone; that person came to thank him; Anshi greatly resented it, thinking that raising the worthy and promoting the capable—how could there be private thanks?
translations.idiomatic: Once he recommended a man for office. When the appointee came to thank him in person, Anshi was furious: recommending talent for the public good was not an occasion for private gratitude.

sentences.zh: 絕井復為通。

translations.literal: He cut him off; afterward again made communication.
translations.idiomatic: He broke with the man entirely, then after a cooling interval allowed the relationship to resume—on his own terms.

sentences.zh: 有郎功高不調，自言，安世應曰：「君之功高，明主所知。

translations.literal: There was a gentleman of merit high who was not transferred; he spoke for himself; Anshi answered, saying, "Sir's merit is high—the enlightened ruler knows it.
translations.idiomatic: A gentleman-attendant of long and distinguished service had not been promoted and spoke up for himself. Anshi replied, "Your record is known to the enlightened ruler.

sentences.zh: 人臣執事，何長短而自言乎！」

translations.literal: A minister holding office—why length and short and speak for oneself!"
translations.idiomatic: A subject does his duty—why should he bargain over seniority in his own voice?"

sentences.zh: 絕不許。

translations.literal: Absolutely he did not permit.
translations.idiomatic: He refused outright.

sentences.zh: 已而郎果遷。

translations.literal: Afterward the gentleman indeed was transferred.
translations.idiomatic: The man was promoted soon afterward in any case.

sentences.zh: 莫府長史遷，辭去之官，安世問以過失。

translations.literal: When the chief clerk of headquarters was transferred, taking leave to go to his post, Anshi asked him concerning faults.
translations.idiomatic: When his headquarters chief clerk was promoted and came to say farewell before leaving for his new command, Anshi asked whether he had ever found fault with his administration.

sentences.zh: 長史曰：「將軍為明主股肱，而士無所進，論者以為譏。」

translations.literal: The chief clerk said, "The general is the enlightened ruler's thigh and arm, yet gentlemen have none whom you advance—those who discuss think it a reproach."
translations.idiomatic: The chief clerk said, "You are the sovereign's right arm, yet you never push candidates forward. Critics call that a fault."

sentences.zh: 安世曰「明主在上，賢不肖較然，臣下自修而已，何知士而薦之？」

translations.literal: Anshi said, "When the enlightened ruler is above, worthy and unworthy are clear; ministers below only cultivate themselves—why know gentlemen and recommend them?"
translations.idiomatic: Anshi answered, "When a clear-sighted ruler sits on the throne, the worthy and the unworthy sort themselves out. Subjects have only to mind their own conduct—why should I play patron to office-seekers?"

sentences.zh: 其欲匿名跡遠權勢如此。

translations.literal: His desire to hide name traces and distance from power and influence was thus.
translations.idiomatic: That was how far he went to efface his own footprint and keep clear of factional power.

sentences.zh: 為光祿勳，郎有醉小便殿上，主事白行法，安世曰：「何以知其不反水漿邪？

translations.literal: As superintendent of the household, a gentleman was drunk and urinated in the hall; the supervisor reported to apply the law; Anshi said, "How do we know he did not spill drink?
translations.idiomatic: While he was superintendent of the household, a gentleman-attendant relieved himself on the palace floor while drunk. The duty officer asked leave to prosecute. Anshi said, "How can we be sure it was not wine he spilled?

sentences.zh: 如何以小過成罪！」

translations.literal: How with a small fault complete a crime!"
translations.idiomatic: Why turn a minor slip into a criminal offense?"

sentences.zh: 郎淫官婢，婢兄自言，安世曰：「奴以恚怒，誣污衣冠。」

translations.literal: A gentleman violated a palace slave girl; the girl's elder brother spoke for himself; Anshi said, "The slave in resentment and anger falsely defiled cap and robe."
translations.idiomatic: Another attendant forced himself on a palace maidservant; her brother laid a complaint. Anshi said, "The girl is a bondservant venting spite—she slanders a man who wears official cap and gown."

sentences.zh: 告署適奴。

translations.literal: He reported and marked him fit for slave.
translations.idiomatic: He had the girl reassigned as a punishment slave.

sentences.zh: 其隱人過失，皆此類也。

translations.literal: His concealing people's faults and errors were all of this kind.
translations.idiomatic: His habit of covering others' missteps ran in this vein throughout his career.

sentences.zh: 安世自見父子尊顯，懷不自安，為子延壽求出補吏，上以為北地太守。

translations.literal: Anshi himself seeing father and son honored and prominent, harbored unease in himself, and for his son Yanshou sought to go out and fill a clerk post; the ruler made him governor of Beidi.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing how exalted he and his father had become, Anshi grew uneasy. He asked that his son Yanshou be given a provincial post, and the emperor appointed Yanshou governor of Beidi.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，上閔安世年老，復徵延壽為左曹、太僕。

translations.literal: After more than a year, the ruler pitied Anshi's old age and again summoned Yanshou as left aide and chamberlain for the imperial stud.
translations.idiomatic: A year later, pitying Anshi's age, the emperor recalled Yanshou to court as left aide in the secretariat and chamberlain for the imperial stud.

sentences.zh: 安世尊為公侯，食邑萬戶，然身衣弋綈，夫人自紡績，家童七百人，皆有手技作事，內治產業，累織纖微，是以能殖其貨，富於大將軍光。

translations.literal: Anshi was honored as duke or marquis, with fief of ten thousand households, yet personally he wore coarse black silk; his wife herself spun and wove; household youths seven hundred persons all had handicraft work; inwardly he managed industry, accumulating stitch by stitch fine detail—thereby he was able to increase his goods, richer than Grand General Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Though ennobled at the highest rank with a fief of ten thousand households, he dressed in plain black silk and his wife spun the household's cloth herself. Seven hundred retainers were each put to skilled manual labor. He micromanaged the family estates, pinching every cash, until his private wealth outstripped even Grand General Huo Guang's.

sentences.zh: 天子甚尊憚大將軍，然內親安世，心密於光焉。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven greatly honored and feared the grand general, yet inwardly was close to Anshi, his heart more secret than toward Guang.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor treated Huo Guang with awe and kept him at a distance, but he was genuinely intimate with Anshi in a way he never was with Guang.

sentences.zh: 安世子 延壽

translations.literal: Anshi's son: Yanshou.
translations.idiomatic: Anshi's son: Zhang Yanshou.

sentences.zh: 延壽子 勃

translations.literal: Yanshou's son: Bo.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou's son: Zhang Bo.

sentences.zh: 勃子 臨

translations.literal: Bo's son: Lin.
translations.idiomatic: Bo's son: Zhang Lin.

sentences.zh: 臨亦謙儉，每登閣殿，常歎曰：「桑、霍為我戒，豈不厚哉！」

translations.literal: Lin also was modest and frugal; each time he ascended the tower hall, he always sighed, saying, "Sang and Huo are my warning—is it not thick!"
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Lin was as modest and frugal as his forebears. Whenever he climbed the towered halls of the palace, he would sigh, "The houses of Sang and Huo are my warning—how stern a lesson that is!"

sentences.zh: 且死，分施宗族故舊，薄葬不起墳。

translations.literal: Nearing death, he divided and gave to clan and old friends, was thinly buried and did not raise a mound.
translations.idiomatic: As death approached, he distributed his goods among kinsmen and old friends and ordered a simple burial with no tumulus.

sentences.zh: 臨尚敬武公主。

translations.literal: Lin married Princess Jingwu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Lin married Princess Jingwu.

sentences.zh: 薨，子放嗣。

translations.literal: He died; his son Fang inherited.
translations.idiomatic: When he died, his son Zhang Fang succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 臨子 放

translations.literal: Lin's son: Fang.
translations.idiomatic: Lin's son: Zhang Fang.

sentences.zh: 鴻嘉中，上欲遵武帝故事，與近臣游宴，放以公主子開敏得幸。

translations.literal: In the Hongjia years, the ruler wished to follow Emperor Wu's precedents and with near ministers roam and feast; Fang by being the princess's son open and clever obtained favor.
translations.idiomatic: During the Hongjia era Emperor Cheng wished to revive Emperor Wu's habit of informal outings and banquets with his inner circle. Zhang Fang, as the princess's son—quick-witted and polished—won exceptional favor.

sentences.zh: 放取皇后弟平恩侯許嘉女，上為放供張，賜甲第，充以乘輿服飾，號為天子取婦，皇后嫁女。

translations.literal: Fang took the daughter of the empress's younger brother Marquis of Ping'en Xu Jia; the ruler for Fang supplied furnishings, granted a first-ranked mansion, filled it with imperial carriage robes and ornaments, calling it the Son of Heaven taking a wife and the empress marrying a daughter.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fang married the daughter of Xu Jia, the Marquis of Ping'en and younger brother of the empress. The emperor staged the wedding himself, granted a mansion of the first rank, and filled it with wardrobe and ornaments from the imperial workshops, so that courtiers quipped it was as if the Son of Heaven had taken a bride and the empress had given her daughter away.

sentences.zh: 大官私官並供其第，兩宮使者冠蓋不絕，賞賜以千萬數。

translations.literal: Great Steward and Private Steward together supplied his mansion; two palaces' envoys caps and covers unbroken; gifts and grants in the tens of millions.
translations.idiomatic: The palace stewards of both households provisioned the estate without stint; couriers from the two palaces arrived in an unbroken stream of carriages; gifts ran into the tens of millions of cash.

sentences.zh: 放為侍中、中郎將，監平樂屯兵，置莫府，儀比將軍。

translations.literal: Fang was palace attendant and general of the household gentlemen, supervised the Pingyue garrison, set up headquarters, ceremony comparable to a general.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fang held concurrent rank as palace attendant and general of the household gentlemen, commanded the encamped troops at Pingyue, maintained a full staff, and enjoyed ceremonial honors equal to a field general.

sentences.zh: 與上臥起，寵愛殊絕，常從為微行出遊，北至甘泉，南至長楊、五莋，鬥雞走馬長安中，積數年。

translations.literal: With the ruler he slept and rose; favor and love were uniquely extreme; he always followed on incognito outings, north to Ganquan, south to Changyang and Wuzuo, cockfighting and horse racing in Chang'an—accumulated several years.
translations.idiomatic: He attended the emperor waking and sleeping and stood alone in favor. He was the constant companion on disguised excursions—north to Ganquan, south to the hunting parks of Changyang and Wuzuo, cockfighting and racing horses through the streets of Chang'an—for years on end.

sentences.zh: 是時，上諸舅皆害其寵，白太后。

translations.literal: At this time the ruler's various maternal uncles all envied his favor and reported to the empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor's uncles on his mother's side grew jealous of his influence and complained to the empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 太后以上春秋富，動作不節，甚以過放。

translations.literal: The empress dowager because the ruler's years were rich in spring, movements lacked restraint, greatly blamed Fang.
translations.idiomatic: She rebuked Zhang Fang harshly, saying the emperor was still young and his conduct at court and abroad lacked restraint.

sentences.zh: 時數有災異，議者歸咎放等。

translations.literal: At that time repeatedly there were disasters and prodigies; those who deliberated attributed guilt to Fang and others.
translations.idiomatic: Omens and disasters multiplied at the time, and memorialists laid the blame at the door of Zhang Fang and his like.

sentences.zh: 於是丞相宣、御史大夫方進奏：「放驕蹇縱恣，奢淫不制。

translations.literal: Thereupon Chancellor Xuan and Imperial Counselor Fangjin memorialized, saying, "Fang is arrogant, remiss, wanton, and unrestrained, extravagant and dissolute without restraint.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Xuan and Imperial Counselor Zhu Fangjin therefore submitted a joint memorial: "Zhang Fang is insolent, overbearing, and utterly without self-control—profligate and debauched beyond measure.

sentences.zh: 前侍御史修等四人奉使至放家逐名捕賊，時放見在，奴從者閉門設兵弩射吏，距使者不肯內。

translations.literal: Formerly attendant censors Xiu and others four men by mission arrived at Fang's house to pursue by name and capture bandits; at the time Fang was present; slave followers closed the gate, set up weapons and crossbows, shot at clerks, and resisted the envoys, refusing to admit them.
translations.idiomatic: When four attendant censors led by Xiu were sent to his house to arrest wanted men by name, Zhang Fang was at home. His slaves barred the gates, strung crossbows, and fired on the officers, defying the imperial messengers and refusing them entry.

sentences.zh: 知男子李游君欲獻女，使樂府音監景武強求不得，使如康等之其家，賊傷三人。

translations.literal: Knowing the man Li Youjun wished to present a daughter, he had the music-house pitch-pipe supervisor Jing Wu forcibly seek and not obtain; he sent such as Ru Kang to their house, and they wounded three persons.
translations.idiomatic: Learning that a commoner named Li Youjun meant to offer a daughter in marriage to the throne, he had Jing Wu, the music bureau's intendant for pitch pipes, try to seize the girl for himself and fail, then sent ruffians such as Ru Kang to Li's house; they left three people wounded.

sentences.zh: 又以縣官事怨樂府游徼莽，而使大奴駿等四十餘人群黨盛兵弩，白晝入樂府攻射官寺，縛束長吏子弟，斫破器物，宮中皆奔走伏匿。

translations.literal: Again on a county-office matter he resented the music-house patrol officer Mang, and sent great slaves Jun and others more than forty men in a crowd with abundant weapons and crossbows; in broad daylight they entered the music house, attacked and shot the official compound, bound the chief clerk's sons and younger brothers, hacked and broke vessels and tools; within the palace all ran and hid.
translations.idiomatic: On another occasion, nursing a grudge against the music-house patrol officer Mang over a county matter, he dispatched more than forty chief slaves led by Jun, armed to the teeth. In full daylight they stormed the music bureau, shot up the yamen, seized the chief clerk's sons and nephews, smashed furniture and equipment, and threw the inner palace into panic.

sentences.zh: 奔自髡鉗，衣赭衣，及守令史調等皆徒跣叩頭謝放，放乃止。

translations.literal: Ben shaved himself and collar, wore ochre clothes, and chief clerk assistant Diao and others all went barefoot and knocked heads apologizing to Fang; Fang then stopped.
translations.idiomatic: The chief clerk's son Ben shaved his head and donned the convict's iron collar and red robe in token of submission, while the yeoman Diao and the other clerks went barefoot to Zhang Fang's gate to kowtow in apology—only then did he call off his men.

sentences.zh: 奴從者支屬並乘權勢為暴虐，至求吏妻不得，殺其夫，或恚一人，妄殺其親屬，輒亡人放弟，不得，幸得勿治。

translations.literal: Slave followers' branch kin all rode power and influence to commit violence and cruelty, to the point of seeking an official's wife and not obtaining it, killing her husband, or resenting one man and recklessly killing his relatives; they always fled into Fang's mansion; when not caught, fortunately they were not prosecuted.
translations.idiomatic: His slaves and their hangers-on traded on his power: they murdered a magistrate whose wife they could not take, slaughtered the kin of anyone who offended them, then took refuge inside Zhang Fang's compound. When the authorities could not lay hands on them, they often walked away unpunished.

sentences.zh: 放行輕薄，連犯大惡，有感動陰陽之咎，為臣不忠首，罪名雖顯，前蒙恩。

translations.literal: Fang's conduct was frivolous; repeatedly he violated great evil; there was a fault of moving yin and yang; as minister unfilial he was chief; though the crime name was manifest, formerly he received grace.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fang's conduct was reckless and he piled up grave crimes, enough to disturb the balance of nature. He stood first among disloyal ministers. His guilt was obvious, yet he had long enjoyed imperial indulgence.

sentences.zh: 驕逸悖理，與背畔無異，臣子之惡，莫大於是，不宜宿衛在位。

translations.literal: Arrogant ease contrary to principle differs not from rebellion; among son-minister evil there is none greater than this—he ought not to lodge guard in position.
translations.idiomatic: Such wanton disregard of duty is little short of treason. No subject's wickedness could exceed this. He must not remain in the palace guard.

sentences.zh: 臣請免放歸國，以銷眾邪之萌，厭海內之心。」

translations.literal: Your servants request to dismiss Fang to his state, in order to dissolve the sprout of many evils and satisfy the mind within the seas."
translations.idiomatic: We beg that Zhang Fang be stripped of office and sent to his fief, to nip this evil in the bud and give the empire what it wants to hear."

sentences.zh: 上不得已，左遷放為北地都尉。

translations.literal: The ruler had no choice; he demoted Fang to be commandant of Beidi.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, unable to refuse outright, relegated Zhang Fang to commandant of Beidi.

sentences.zh: 數月，復徵入侍中。

translations.literal: After several months, again summoned in as palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Within a few months he was recalled to serve again as palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 太后以放為言，出放為天水屬國都尉。

translations.literal: The empress dowager spoke of Fang; he sent Fang out to be commandant of the dependent state of Tianshui.
translations.idiomatic: At the empress dowager's insistence he was transferred out again, this time as commandant of the Tianshui dependent state.

sentences.zh: 永始、元延間，比年日蝕，故久不還放，璽書勞問不絕。

translations.literal: Between Yongshi and Yuanyan, year after year solar eclipses; therefore long he did not return Fang; sealed edicts labor inquiries unbroken.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yongshi and Yuanyan reign periods eclipses came year after year, so the emperor kept Zhang Fang away from the capital for a long stretch, though edicts of inquiry and reassurance followed him without cease.

sentences.zh: 居歲餘，徵放歸第視母公主疾。

translations.literal: Dwelling more than a year, he summoned Fang to return to his mansion to view his mother the princess's illness.
translations.idiomatic: After more than a year abroad he was allowed home to his mansion to nurse his mother, the princess, in her illness.

sentences.zh: 數月，主有瘳，出放為何東都尉。

translations.literal: After several months, the princess had recovery; he sent Fang out to be commandant of Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: When she had recovered several months later, he posted Zhang Fang as commandant of Hedong.

sentences.zh: 上雖愛放，然上迫太后，下用大臣，故常涕泣而遣之。

translations.literal: The ruler although loved Fang, yet above was pressed by the empress dowager, below employed great ministers—therefore often wept tears and sent him away.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor still loved Zhang Fang, but he could not withstand pressure from the empress dowager above and his ministers below, and more than once he dismissed him with tears in his eyes.

sentences.zh: 後復徵放為侍中光祿大夫，秩中二千石。

translations.literal: Afterward again summoned Fang as palace attendant and grand coachman, rank two thousand piculs at middle grade.
translations.idiomatic: Later he recalled Zhang Fang to serve as palace attendant and grand coachman at the middle rank of two thousand piculs.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，丞相方進復奏放，上不得已，免放，賜錢五百萬，遣就國。

translations.literal: After more than a year, Chancellor Fangjin again memorialized Fang; the ruler had no choice; dismissed Fang, granted cash five million, sent to go to his state.
translations.idiomatic: A year later Chancellor Zhu Fangjin renewed the attack. The emperor had no choice but to dismiss Zhang Fang, grant him five million cash, and order him to his fief.

sentences.zh: 數月，成帝崩，放思慕哭泣而死。

translations.literal: After several months, Emperor Cheng died; Fang thought longing and wept and died.
translations.idiomatic: A few months later Emperor Cheng died. Zhang Fang grieved so deeply that he wept himself to death.

sentences.zh: 初，安世長子千秋與霍光子禹俱為中郎將，將兵隨度遼將軍范明友擊烏桓。

translations.literal: At first, Anshi's eldest son Qianqiu and Huo Guang's son Yu together were generals of the household gentlemen, leading troops following the Trans-Liao general Fan Mingyou to strike the Wuhuan.
translations.idiomatic: Long before, Anshi's eldest son Zhang Qianqiu and Huo Guang's son Huo Yu had both been generals of the household gentlemen when they campaigned under the Trans-Liao general Fan Mingyou against the Wuhuan.

sentences.zh: 還，謁大將軍光，問千秋戰鬥方略，山川形勢，千秋口對兵事，畫地成圖，無所忘失。

translations.literal: Returning, they visited Grand General Guang; Guang asked Qianqiu battle methods and plans, mountains and rivers configuration; Qianqiu orally replied on military affairs, drew on the ground and made a map, nothing forgotten or lost.
translations.idiomatic: On their return they called on Huo Guang, who questioned Qianqiu about tactics and terrain. Qianqiu answered from memory, sketching the campaign map in the dust without a single error.

sentences.zh: 光復問禹，禹不能記，曰：「皆有文書。」

translations.literal: Guang again asked Yu; Yu could not remember, saying, "All have documents."
translations.idiomatic: When he put the same questions to Huo Yu, Yu could not recall a thing and said, "It is all on file somewhere."

sentences.zh: 光由是賢千秋，以禹為不材，歎曰：「霍氏世衰，張氏興矣！」

translations.literal: Guang from this deemed Qianqiu worthy and Yu not talented, sighed, saying, "The Huo clan generation declines; the Zhang clan rises!"
translations.idiomatic: From that day Huo Guang respected Qianqiu and wrote Yu off as worthless, sighing, "The house of Huo is fading; the house of Zhang is on the rise!"

sentences.zh: 及禹誅滅，而安世子孫相繼，自宣、元以來為侍中、中常侍、諸曹散騎、列校尉者凡十餘人。

translations.literal: When Yu was exterminated and destroyed, yet Anshi's sons and grandsons succeeded one another, from Emperors Xuan and Yuan onward as palace attendants, regular palace attendants, various bureau mounted escorts, and colonels—more than ten persons in all.
translations.idiomatic: After the Huo clan was extirpated, Zhang Anshi's descendants held one high post after another. From the reigns of Xuandi and Yuandi onward, more than ten of them served as palace attendants, regular attendants, mounted escorts in the various bureaus, or colonels of the guard.

sentences.zh: 功臣之世，唯有金氏、張氏，親近寵貴，比於外戚。

translations.literal: Among meritorious minister generations, only the Jin clan and Zhang clan were intimate, favored, and honored, comparable to affines by marriage.
translations.idiomatic: Of all the families ennobled for merit, only the Jins and the Zhangs remained so close to the throne—almost like imperial in-laws.

sentences.zh: 放子 純

translations.literal: Fang's son: Chun.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fang's son: Zhang Chun.

sentences.zh: 放子純嗣侯，恭儉自修，明習漢家制度故事，有敬侯遺風。

translations.literal: Fang's son Chun inherited the marquisate, was respectful and frugal and cultivated himself, clearly practiced Han house system precedents, having the Marquis of Jing's surviving manner.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chun, Zhang Fang's son, succeeded to the marquisate. He was modest, frugal, and self-disciplined, thoroughly versed in Han institutions and precedent, and carried something of the old Marquis Jing's manner.

sentences.zh: 王莽時不失爵，建武中歷位至大司空，更封富平之別鄉為武始侯。

translations.literal: In Wang Mang's time he did not lose the noble; in the Jianwu years he passed through offices to grand minister of works, again enfeoffing a separate village of Fuping as Marquis of Wushi.
translations.idiomatic: He kept his title even under Wang Mang, and in the Jianwu era rose step by step to grand minister of works; Guangwudi carved a detached village out of the old Fuping fief and re-enfeoffed him as marquis of Wushi.

sentences.zh: 張湯本居杜陵，安世武、昭、宣世輒隨陵，凡三徙，復還杜陵。

translations.literal: Zhang Tang originally dwelt at Duling; Anshi in the Wu, Zhao, and Xuan generations always followed the mausoleum sites; in all three moves, again returned to Duling.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhangs had begun at Duling. Zhang Anshi's household moved with each imperial tomb town under Wudi, Zhaodi, and Xuandi—three relocations in all—before returning to Duling.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：馮商稱張湯之先與留侯同祖，而司馬遷不言，故闕焉。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Feng Shang claimed Zhang Tang's ancestors shared the same ancestor as the Marquis of Liu, but Sima Qian does not speak of it—therefore it is omitted.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's comment: Feng Shang claimed that Zhang Tang's line shared an ancestor with the Marquis of Liu, but Sima Qian does not say so, and we leave the point aside.

sentences.zh: 漢興以來，侯者百數，保國持寵，未有若富平者也。

translations.literal: Since Han rose, those enfeoffed as marquis have been in the hundreds; to preserve the state and maintain favor, none has been like Fuping.
translations.idiomatic: Since the founding of Han, hundreds of families have received marquisates, yet none has held its fortune and imperial favor as steadily as Fuping.

sentences.zh: 湯雖酷烈，及身蒙咎，其推賢揚善，固宜有後。

translations.literal: Although Tang was cruel and fierce, reaching himself covered with blame, his pushing the worthy and displaying the good—solidly he ought to have posterity.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Tang was a cruel judge and came to a bad end himself, yet in promoting able men and speaking well of others he earned the posterity he deserved.

sentences.zh: 安世履道，滿而不溢。

translations.literal: Anshi trod the Way, full yet not overflowing.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Anshi walked the middle path—his cup was full, yet he never let it spill.

sentences.zh: 賀之陰德，亦有助云。

translations.literal: He's hidden virtue—there was also help.
translations.idiomatic: The quiet kindness Zhang He once showed the future Emperor Xuan also played its part in the family's lasting good fortune.
